sources_audited.note: The cited Census profile resolves to the NAICS 238330 Flooring Contractors page, but the reopened response is JavaScript-only, so the detailed inclusion and exclusion language could not be text-verified from the rendered audit response. The primitive is already labeled ESTIMATE and does not claim the page measures eligibility.

The May 2023 OEWS page is explicitly for parent NAICS 238300 and reports the cited occupational shares, including construction and extraction at 70.96%, management at 5.32%, business and financial operations at 4.63%, and office support at 8.59%.

O*NET documents the cited preparation, cutting, fitting, adhesion, and finishing tasks, reports 89% continual or near-continual hand use, and lists flooring estimating and project-management software. It supports the task facts, not a measured AI-exposure or five-year persistence share, as the bridges acknowledge.

The Census BTOS article reports a 19.8% national AI-use rate as of May 3, 2026, less than 20% use among firms with four or fewer employees, and the stated all-nonfarm, any-business-function survey scope.

The CoConstruct article states that builders used fixed-price contracts 80% of the time, analyzes more than 60,000 U.S. residential projects from 2018 through 2020, and distinguishes fixed-price from open-book contracts. It does not directly measure flooring-contractor AI-benefit retention, a limitation explicitly disclosed in q.

The Census infographic reports 51% age 55 or older and identifies the 2019 Annual Business Survey, data year 2018; it also states that estimates cover 4.1 million responding owners of employer businesses rather than all U.S. owners.

BizBuySell reports 3,142 sold listings analyzed, a median 207 days on market, inclusion of flooring among represented construction specialties, and that transaction data are based on businesses sold on or reported to the marketplace.

BLS describes the cited physical duties, projects 6% aggregate employment growth from 2024 to 2034, identifies new homes and renovation as primary demand sources, and reports divergent outlooks for carpet installers, resilient floor layers, and tile and stone setters.

caveats: S1's JavaScript-only audit response prevented direct verification of the detailed NAICS inclusion and exclusion wording; this does not change the ESTIMATE classification of e, and a correction would not change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action.

The a and rho bases remain judgmental transformations from a parent-industry occupation mix, one floor-layer occupation, and cross-industry AI adoption; the bridges disclose the population and construct gaps, but no firm-level flooring workflow evidence anchors the selected bases.

The q source supports residential-builder contract prevalence and terminology, not a five-year retention rate for AI benefits at flooring contractors; the selected q range is therefore substantially model-conditioned despite its honest PROXY label.

The s5 estimate combines dated all-industry owner-age evidence with selected marketplace transactions that have no flooring-specific population denominator, so it demonstrates aging pressure and transaction feasibility rather than a five-year control-transfer probability.

The d5 and o bridges convert occupational projections and current physical duties into service-quantity and operator-persistence estimates; inclusion of tile and stone occupations outside NAICS 238330 and the absence of direct quantity forecasts limit precision.
summary: Mechanics pass. The reopened sources support the cited factual starting points, apart from partial auditability of the JavaScript-dependent Census NAICS profile. Evidence states, bridges, lens, ranges, and narrative are candid about the substantial transformations from broad proxies to flooring-specific primitives. Those weaknesses warrant caveats, but none indicates a correction or honest evidence-state downgrade that would change the STRUCTURAL_OUT base tier or the already MODEL_CONDITIONED VALIDATE_ASSUMPTIONS action; there is therefore no material finding under the frozen test.
